turns.content: I'm trying to write a driver for an LED on my board, but I keep forgetting that we don't have std. Imagine I want to blink the onboard LED using the digital output trait from embedded-hal. If I try to use println! inside my blink function, it will panic because there's no standard library available on this chip.

Exactly right. Since you are targeting a microcontroller without the Rust standard library, your driver cannot rely on any std features like println! or heap allocation. You must implement your blink logic using only the traits provided by embedded-hal, specifically the digital output trait which exposes methods like set_high and set_low to control the pin.

Okay so I just call those methods directly? But what if my hardware is different? Like maybe it has an SPI bus instead of a simple digital pin for communication. Does the abstraction layer handle that automatically?

Not exactly automatically, but the embedded-hal crate provides a unified trait system so you write code once and it adapts to different hardware backends. For SPI, there is a separate set of traits like spi_write that handle the bus protocol for you. You implement your driver against these traits instead of hardcoding register addresses.

That sounds convenient but I'm worried about testability. If I just call abstract functions, how do I verify my code works without actually connecting to a physical chip? Is there a way to mock these traits?

You can absolutely mock them by creating fake implementations of the same trait interfaces. Rust allows you to write unit tests that depend on those traits, so your driver logic only cares about the trait contract. You can build a test harness with dummy hardware that satisfies the spi_write or digital output trait requirements without needing real hardware.
Wait, I think I misunderstood something earlier. So when I say 'no_std', does that mean I literally can't use any standard library functions at all? Like even basic math operations?

You are correct about the strict definition of no_std environments, but in practice you only need to avoid std features like panic or dynamic memory allocation. Basic arithmetic is built into the core language itself via traits like Add and Mul, so you can still do math without importing any external crates.
